title: warp::fs::file does not percent-decode paths passed directly to file()
body: fs.rs's file() filter always serves the exact PathBuf given, but dir() decodes the request path via percent_decode_str before joining it to the base directory. When combining file() with a path segment extracted from the URL manually (a common pattern shown in examples/dir.rs), percent-encoded characters like %2e%2e aren't decoded consistently between the two entry points, which is confusing and could lead to subtle path handling bugs.

title: cors() Builder has no way to reflect a dynamic allow-list of origins
body: warp::cors() Builder only supports allow_origin/allow_origins with static strings or allow_any_origin(); there's no hook for validating the incoming Origin header against, say, a database-backed list of tenant domains at request time. Every app doing multi-tenant CORS ends up wrapping the whole filter in a custom rejection instead.

title: IfRange handling in fs.rs can return a full 200 body instead of a 206 partial
body: file_reply / conditionals() in fs.rs use IfModifiedSince/IfUnmodifiedSince/IfRange/Range headers to decide between a full response and a ranged one, but when If-Range references an ETag/date that doesn't match, the current logic falls through to serving the full file with a 200 rather than explicitly re-validating and returning 206 for the requested range once conditions are met, per RFC 9110.

title: examples/sse_chat.rs disconnect isn't reflected in websockets_chat.rs's user list
body: Comparing examples/sse_chat.rs and examples/websockets_chat.rs, the websocket example properly removes a user from the shared Users map on disconnect, but the equivalent broadcast cleanup isn't demonstrated for SSE clients, so copying the SSE example as a starting point leaks stale client channels over time.

title: warp::reject::custom rejections lose their type when combined via or()
body: reject.rs's Rejection::find::<T>() requires the exact concrete type of a custom rejection, but when two filters combined with .or() each reject with different custom error types, recover() handlers written against one type silently miss rejections produced by the other branch, and end up falling through to the default 404/500 handling instead of the intended custom response.
